candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Enhancing Perceptual Quality in Video Super-Resolution through
  Temporally-Consistent Detail Synthesis using Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
In this paper, we address the problem of enhancing perceptual quality in
video super-resolution (VSR) using Diffusion Models (DMs) while ensuring
temporal consistency among frames. We present StableVSR, a VSR method based on
DMs that can significantly enhance the perceptual quality of upscaled videos by
synthesizing realistic and temporally-consistent details. We introduce the
Temporal Conditioning Module (TCM) into a pre-trained DM for single image
super-resolution to turn it into a VSR method. TCM uses the novel Temporal
Texture Guidance, which provides it with spatially-aligned and detail-rich
texture information synthesized in adjacent frames. This guides the generative
process of the current frame toward high-quality and temporally-consistent
results. In addition, we introduce the novel Frame-wise Bidirectional Sampling
strategy to encourage the use of information from past to future and
vice-versa. This strategy improves the perceptual quality of the results and
the temporal consistency across frames. We demonstrate the effectiveness of
StableVSR in enhancing the perceptual quality of upscaled videos while
achieving better temporal consistency compared to existing state-of-the-art
methods for VSR. The project page is available at
https://github.com/claudiom4sir/StableVSR.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper presents StableVSR, a novel approach to video super-resolution that enhances perceptual quality by ensuring temporal consistency among frames, addressing the limitations of existing VSR methods that focus primarily on reconstruction quality.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: StableVSR is built on Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) and introduces a Temporal Conditioning Module (TCM) that employs Temporal Texture Guidance for synthesizing detail-rich textures from adjacent frames. A Frame-wise Bidirectional Sampling strategy is implemented to propagate information forward and backward across frames.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated using two benchmark datasets: Vimeo-90K containing 91701 7-frame video sequences, and REDS, which includes 300 video sequences. Perceptual quality was assessed using metrics like LPIPS and DISTS, alongside traditional metrics such as PSNR and SSIM.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: StableVSR exhibits a significantly higher model complexity, approximately 35 times greater than compared methods, resulting in increased inference time (about 100 seconds per frame) and higher memory requirements. Improvements in perceptual quality may come at the cost of reconstruction quality, adhering to the perception-distortion trade-off.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work may focus on reducing sampling steps to enhance inference speed, exploring optimizations for the diffusion models, and investigating other applications of StableVSR in fields requiring high perceptual quality, such as film and gaming.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title: Diffusion dynamics of star-shaped macromolecules in dilute solutions
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Polymer chains dissolved in a solvent take random conformations due to large
internal degrees of freedom and are characterized geometrically by their
average shape and size. The diffusive dynamics of such large macromolecules
play an indispensable role in a plethora of engineering applications. The
influence of the size of the polymer chain on its diffusion is well studied,
whereas the same cannot be said for the shape of the polymer chain. In the
present work, the influence of shape on the center-of-mass diffusion of the
star-shaped chains in solution is investigated using Multi-particle Collision
Dynamics. Star-shaped chains of varying degrees of functionality are modeled in
a good solvent at infinite dilution. The radius of gyration($R_g$) of the
star-shaped chains follows a functionality-independent scaling law with the
chain length($N$), $R_g \sim N^{\nu}$, where $\nu \sim 0.627$. The shape of the
polymer chains is calibrated by relative shape anisotropy. Highly anisotropic
star-shaped polymer chains are found to have a faster rate of diffusion along
the translational direction due to a slower rate of rotational diffusion when
the radius of gyration of the polymer chains is maintained constant.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research investigates the effect of shape on the center-of-mass diffusion of star-shaped polymer chains in dilute solutions, revealing that highly anisotropic chains diffuse faster due to lower rotational diffusion rates while maintaining a constant radius of gyration. It establishes a scaling law for the radius of gyration independent of functionality, with an exponent of approximately 0.627.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The study employs Multi-particle Collision Dynamics (MPCD) for simulating the Brownian motion of star-shaped polymer chains. The polymer chains are modeled using a coarse-grained bead-spring model, with suitable potentials to replicate behavior in a good solvent.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Simulations were conducted in a cubic box of varying sizes depending on polymer length, ensuring infinite dilution conditions. The diffusion coefficients were computed from the center-of-mass mean square displacement over time for different chain types, while the rotational diffusion was evaluated using the reorientation correlation function.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The study does not explore the effects of varying solvent quality or concentration on the diffusion behavior of polymers, potentially limiting the generalization of findings across different conditions.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Exploration of other polymer architectures and shapes is suggested to validate the findings further, along with potential investigations into the effects of solvent interactions and crowding on polymer diffusion.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Post-training Quantization for Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with
  Progressive Calibration and Activation Relaxing
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
High computational overhead is a troublesome problem for diffusion models.
Recent studies have leveraged post-training quantization (PTQ) to compress
diffusion models. However, most of them only focus on unconditional models,
leaving the quantization of widely-used pretrained text-to-image models, e.g.,
Stable Diffusion, largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose a novel
post-training quantization method PCR (Progressive Calibration and Relaxing)
for text-to-image diffusion models, which consists of a progressive calibration
strategy that considers the accumulated quantization error across timesteps,
and an activation relaxing strategy that improves the performance with
negligible cost. Additionally, we demonstrate the previous metrics for
text-to-image diffusion model quantization are not accurate due to the
distribution gap. To tackle the problem, we propose a novel QDiffBench
benchmark, which utilizes data in the same domain for more accurate evaluation.
Besides, QDiffBench also considers the generalization performance of the
quantized model outside the calibration dataset. Extensive experiments on
Stable Diffusion and Stable Diffusion XL demonstrate the superiority of our
method and benchmark. Moreover, we are the first to achieve quantization for
Stable Diffusion XL while maintaining the performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research addresses the high computational cost of text-to-image diffusion models through a novel post-training quantization method named Progressive Calibration and Relaxing (PCR). This method incorporates a progressive calibration strategy to account for accumulated quantization error across denoising timesteps and an activation relaxing strategy to enhance performance with minimal cost. The paper also introduces QDiffBench, a new benchmark aimed at improving the accuracy of quantization evaluation for these models.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology consists of a progressive calibration strategy that quantizes activations at each timestep considering previous steps' quantized outputs, and an activation relaxing strategy that utilizes higher bit-width for sensitive timesteps to enhance fidelity or text-image matching. These techniques are designed to overcome the limitations of existing quantization methods that ignore time-dependent errors and distribution gaps.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized two datasets: the COCO dataset for calibration and evaluation and the Stable-Diffusion-Prompts dataset to test the prompt-generalization ability of the models. The evaluation metrics included FID to FP32 to measure the quality of generated images relative to those from the full-precision model, and CLIP scores to assess text-image matching. The baseline methods for comparison included Q-diffusion and PTQ4DM, tested under various quantization configurations (e.g., W4/A8, W8/A8).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: Limitations include the assumption that the calibration dataset is representative of user prompts, potential overfitting to the calibration dataset, and the reliance on existing benchmarks which may not adequately encapsulate the performance degradation of quantized models.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on refining the QDiffBench benchmark, exploring further optimizations in quantization techniques, and expanding the methods for different types of generative models beyond text-to-image diffusion. Implications of improved quantization strategies on real-time applications and integration with different hardware platforms could also be significant.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title: Concept Sliders: LoRA Adaptors for Precise Control in Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
We present a method to create interpretable concept sliders that enable
precise control over attributes in image generations from diffusion models. Our
approach identifies a low-rank parameter direction corresponding to one concept
while minimizing interference with other attributes. A slider is created using
a small set of prompts or sample images; thus slider directions can be created
for either textual or visual concepts. Concept Sliders are plug-and-play: they
can be composed efficiently and continuously modulated, enabling precise
control over image generation. In quantitative experiments comparing to
previous editing techniques, our sliders exhibit stronger targeted edits with
lower interference. We showcase sliders for weather, age, styles, and
expressions, as well as slider compositions. We show how sliders can transfer
latents from StyleGAN for intuitive editing of visual concepts for which
textual description is difficult. We also find that our method can help address
persistent quality issues in Stable Diffusion XL including repair of object
deformations and fixing distorted hands. Our code, data, and trained sliders
are available at https://sliders.baulab.info/
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces Concept Sliders, a method that enables precise control over attributes in image generations from diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion. It identifies low-rank directions in parameter space corresponding to specific concepts while minimizing interference with other attributes, allowing effective image editing and attribute manipulation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The approach involves fine-tuning Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adaptors in diffusion models to create sliders that enhance specific attributes while suppressing others. This is achieved by a guided score that modifies model likelihoods through gradients derived from prompts or images.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) and involve generating edited images based on a diverse set of 30 text-based concepts. The study includes validation of the method's effectiveness through comparative analysis against baseline methods, measuring change using metrics like CLIP score and LPIPS distance, and human perceptual studies.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: There is some residual interference between edits despite the disentanglement objective, indicating a need for improved selection of preservation directions. Additionally, the SDEdit technique may reduce edit intensity, suggesting a trade-off between structural coherence and editing strength.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore automated methods for selecting latent directions to minimize interference, enhance the strength and fidelity of edits while using SDEdit, and investigate additional applications of Concept Sliders in various generative model contexts.

Enhancing Perceptual Quality in Video Super-Resolution through

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Enhancing Semantic Fidelity in Text-to-Image Synthesis: Attention
  Regulation in Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Recent advancements in diffusion models have notably improved the perceptual
quality of generated images in text-to-image synthesis tasks. However,
diffusion models often struggle to produce images that accurately reflect the
intended semantics of the associated text prompts. We examine cross-attention
layers in diffusion models and observe a propensity for these layers to
disproportionately focus on certain tokens during the generation process,
thereby undermining semantic fidelity. To address the issue of dominant
attention, we introduce attention regulation, a computation-efficient
on-the-fly optimization approach at inference time to align attention maps with
the input text prompt. Notably, our method requires no additional training or
fine-tuning and serves as a plug-in module on a model. Hence, the generation
capacity of the original model is fully preserved. We compare our approach with
alternative approaches across various datasets, evaluation metrics, and
diffusion models. Experiment results show that our method consistently
outperforms other baselines, yielding images that more faithfully reflect the
desired concepts with reduced computation overhead. Code is available at
https://github.com/YaNgZhAnG-V5/attention_regulation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This research addresses the problem of semantic fidelity in text-to-image generation using diffusion models. The main contribution is the introduction of an on-the-fly attention regulation technique that modifies cross-attention maps at inference time to promote better alignment with text prompts, thereby enhancing the semantic accuracy of generated images without requiring additional training.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The core method involves a constrained optimization approach applied to the attention maps within diffusion models, specifically targeting cross-attention layers. The optimization aims to equalize attention across relevant tokens and mitigate dominance of any single token, utilizing gradient-based techniques for adjustments while preserving the model's original generation capabilities.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized three datasets: a modified subset of the MS-COCO dataset for general evaluation, an A&E dataset specifically designed to measure semantic issues, and a fine-tuned FID dataset for generative quality assessment. Multiple metrics were used for evaluation including CLIP score, detection success rate, LPIPS score, and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID). Comparisons were made across baseline methods with results indicating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method may produce unintended results where objects are fused inappropriately, or certain prompts are interpreted incorrectly due to modeling limitations. Additionally, optimization hyperparameters may lead to performance plateaus or over-editing in attention maps, resulting in potential distortion of final generated images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future exploration could involve improving the interaction and adaptation of attention regulation for more complex image compositions, refining hyperparameter optimization techniques, and assessing the impact on other diffusion model architectures beyond those studied.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Source Prompt Disentangled Inversion for Boosting Image Editability with
  Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Text-driven diffusion models have significantly advanced the image editing
performance by using text prompts as inputs. One crucial step in text-driven
image editing is to invert the original image into a latent noise code
conditioned on the source prompt. While previous methods have achieved
promising results by refactoring the image synthesizing process, the inverted
latent noise code is tightly coupled with the source prompt, limiting the image
editability by target text prompts. To address this issue, we propose a novel
method called Source Prompt Disentangled Inversion (SPDInv), which aims at
reducing the impact of source prompt, thereby enhancing the text-driven image
editing performance by employing diffusion models. To make the inverted noise
code be independent of the given source prompt as much as possible, we indicate
that the iterative inversion process should satisfy a fixed-point constraint.
Consequently, we transform the inversion problem into a searching problem to
find the fixed-point solution, and utilize the pre-trained diffusion models to
facilitate the searching process. The experimental results show that our
proposed SPDInv method can effectively mitigate the conflicts between the
target editing prompt and the source prompt, leading to a significant decrease
in editing artifacts. In addition to text-driven image editing, with SPDInv we
can easily adapt customized image generation models to localized editing tasks
and produce promising performance. The source code are available at
https://github.com/leeruibin/SPDInv.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents the Source Prompt Disentangled Inversion (SPDInv) method, aimed at enhancing text-driven image editing by detaching the inverted latent noise code from the source prompt, thereby improving editability and reducing artifacts in the generated images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: SPDInv reformulates the image inversion process into a fixed-point constraint problem and utilizes pre-trained diffusion models to optimize the inversion without the source prompt prior. It employs iterative optimization to minimize the noise gap between the inverted and ideal noise codes.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments use two datasets: PIE-Bench for general image editing tasks and TDE-Bench for evaluating text-driven editing types. Metrics employed for evaluation include DINO score, PSNR, LPIPS, MSE, SSIM, and CLIP score compared against several state-of-the-art methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: SPDInv is limited by its dependency on existing editing engines, inherits their limitations, and struggles with certain tasks like adding and dropping contents, especially in portrait editing. It reduces but does not eliminate the noise gap.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes improving the SPDInv inversion process and developing new editing pipelines to enhance stability and robustness in image editing tasks.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Exploring Attribute Variations in Style-based GANs using Diffusion
  Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Existing attribute editing methods treat semantic attributes as binary,
resulting in a single edit per attribute. However, attributes such as
eyeglasses, smiles, or hairstyles exhibit a vast range of diversity. In this
work, we formulate the task of \textit{diverse attribute editing} by modeling
the multidimensional nature of attribute edits. This enables users to generate
multiple plausible edits per attribute. We capitalize on disentangled latent
spaces of pretrained GANs and train a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model
(DDPM) to learn the latent distribution for diverse edits. Specifically, we
train DDPM over a dataset of edit latent directions obtained by embedding image
pairs with a single attribute change. This leads to latent subspaces that
enable diverse attribute editing. Applying diffusion in the highly compressed
latent space allows us to model rich distributions of edits within limited
computational resources. Through extensive qualitative and quantitative
experiments conducted across a range of datasets, we demonstrate the
effectiveness of our approach for diverse attribute editing. We also showcase
the results of our method applied for 3D editing of various face attributes.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper addresses the problem of diverse attribute editing in images generated by GANs by proposing a method that learns the distribution of plausible attribute edits rather than treating attributes as binary. The key contributions include a novel methodology utilizing Diffusion Models to generate multiple variations for attributes like eyeglasses and smiles, and demonstrating effectiveness in both 2D and 3D contexts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves training a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) over a dataset of edit directions derived from image pairs differing by a single attribute. The approach capitalizes on disentangled latent spaces of pretrained GANs, specifically using the W+ space for capturing the complex distributions of attribute edits and enabling multiple variations during image editing.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using a synthetic dataset generated from CelebA-HQ, which includes 30k source images to create pairs of images for each attribute. The model was evaluated through qualitative and quantitative metrics like FID, cosine similarity, and improved precision and recall across several attributes including hairstyles, smiles, and eyeglasses, with comparisons made against baseline and FLAME methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The methodology relies on synthetic image pairs for training, which may restrict the model's effectiveness in real-world scenarios. There is also an implicit assumption that attributes can be distinctly altered in isolation, which may not hold true for all images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore text-driven multimodal editing, enabling more complex edits that incorporate not just visual attributes but also contextual information. Additionally, fine-grained control over multimodal attribute editing presents an interesting area for further investigation.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
HiDiffusion: Unlocking Higher-Resolution Creativity and Efficiency in
  Pretrained Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have become a mainstream approach for high-resolution image
synthesis. However, directly generating higher-resolution images from
pretrained diffusion models will encounter unreasonable object duplication and
exponentially increase the generation time. In this paper, we discover that
object duplication arises from feature duplication in the deep blocks of the
U-Net. Concurrently, We pinpoint the extended generation times to
self-attention redundancy in U-Net's top blocks. To address these issues, we
propose a tuning-free higher-resolution framework named HiDiffusion.
Specifically, HiDiffusion contains Resolution-Aware U-Net (RAU-Net) that
dynamically adjusts the feature map size to resolve object duplication and
engages Modified Shifted Window Multi-head Self-Attention (MSW-MSA) that
utilizes optimized window attention to reduce computations. we can integrate
HiDiffusion into various pretrained diffusion models to scale image generation
resolutions even to 4096x4096 at 1.5-6x the inference speed of previous
methods. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach can address object
duplication and heavy computation issues, achieving state-of-the-art
performance on higher-resolution image synthesis tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research introduces HiDiffusion, a tuning-free framework designed to enhance the resolution and efficiency of pretrained diffusion models for image synthesis. It addresses the problems of unreasonable object duplication and excessive generation time when creating high-resolution images, particularly focused on the U-Net architecture used in diffusion models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: HiDiffusion employs a modified U-Net structure called RAU-Net (Resolution-Aware U-Net) to adjust feature maps dynamically to prevent feature duplication, and introduces Modified Shifted Window Multi-head Self-Attention (MSW-MSA) to optimize computation and improve efficiency during image generation without requiring additional training or fine-tuning.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The methodology is evaluated using diffusion models like Stable Diffusion 1.5, 2.1, SDXL Turbo, and SDXL, with experiments performed across varying image resolutions up to 4096×4096. Performance metrics include FID (Frechet Inception Distance), pFID, and CLIP score, based on datasets such as ImageNet and COCO. A total of 10K images were generated for quantitative evaluation, with latency measured on NVIDIA V100 hardware.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method relies on the limitations inherent to the pretrained models it builds upon, such as the need for prompt engineering to achieve optimal results. Further, while the proposed changes improve performance, they may still not fully address inherent image quality issues during generation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on integrating HiDiffusion with advanced super-resolution techniques for even better image quality, exploring more robust prompts for generating images, and investigating the scalability of diffusion models further.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
DGInStyle: Domain-Generalizable Semantic Segmentation with Image
  Diffusion Models and Stylized Semantic Control
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Large, pretrained latent diffusion models (LDMs) have demonstrated an
extraordinary ability to generate creative content, specialize to user data
through few-shot fine-tuning, and condition their output on other modalities,
such as semantic maps. However, are they usable as large-scale data generators,
e.g., to improve tasks in the perception stack, like semantic segmentation? We
investigate this question in the context of autonomous driving, and answer it
with a resounding "yes". We propose an efficient data generation pipeline
termed DGInStyle. First, we examine the problem of specializing a pretrained
LDM to semantically-controlled generation within a narrow domain. Second, we
propose a Style Swap technique to endow the rich generative prior with the
learned semantic control. Third, we design a Multi-resolution Latent Fusion
technique to overcome the bias of LDMs towards dominant objects. Using
DGInStyle, we generate a diverse dataset of street scenes, train a
domain-agnostic semantic segmentation model on it, and evaluate the model on
multiple popular autonomous driving datasets. Our approach consistently
increases the performance of several domain generalization methods compared to
the previous state-of-the-art methods. The source code and the generated
dataset are available at https://dginstyle.github.io.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents DGInStyle, a novel data generation pipeline for improving domain generalization in semantic segmentation, specifically for autonomous driving. It effectively utilizes latent diffusion models to generate a diverse dataset by combining semantic masks and style prompts, leading to significant performance improvements in various domain generalization methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Key techniques include a Style Swap process to decouple semantic control from domain style, and a Multi-Resolution Latent Fusion approach to enhance the generation of small objects. The paper employs latent diffusion models conditioned on segmentation masks to produce aligned images and labels for training.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves generating a dataset from the GTA synthetic source with 24,966 images and evaluating the model across five real-world datasets: Cityscapes, BDD100K, Mapillary Vistas, ACDC, and Dark Zurich. Performance metrics are based on mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) using various domain generalization methods and benchmarks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach inherits the slow inference times associated with diffusion models and requires significant computational resources during the multi-resolution generation process, which may hinder practical application.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on optimizing diffusion model sampling times, exploring further generative enhancements for rare class representation, or applying the framework to other computer vision tasks beyond semantic segmentation.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
To Generate or Not? Safety-Driven Unlearned Diffusion Models Are Still
  Easy To Generate Unsafe Images ... For Now
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
The recent advances in diffusion models (DMs) have revolutionized the
generation of realistic and complex images. However, these models also
introduce potential safety hazards, such as producing harmful content and
infringing data copyrights. Despite the development of safety-driven unlearning
techniques to counteract these challenges, doubts about their efficacy persist.
To tackle this issue, we introduce an evaluation framework that leverages
adversarial prompts to discern the trustworthiness of these safety-driven DMs
after they have undergone the process of unlearning harmful concepts.
Specifically, we investigated the adversarial robustness of DMs, assessed by
adversarial prompts, when eliminating unwanted concepts, styles, and objects.
We develop an effective and efficient adversarial prompt generation approach
for DMs, termed UnlearnDiffAtk. This method capitalizes on the intrinsic
classification abilities of DMs to simplify the creation of adversarial
prompts, thereby eliminating the need for auxiliary classification or diffusion
models. Through extensive benchmarking, we evaluate the robustness of
widely-used safety-driven unlearned DMs (i.e., DMs after unlearning undesirable
concepts, styles, or objects) across a variety of tasks. Our results
demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency merits of UnlearnDiffAtk over the
state-of-the-art adversarial prompt generation method and reveal the lack of
robustness of current safetydriven unlearning techniques when applied to DMs.
Codes are available at https://github.com/OPTML-Group/Diffusion-MU-Attack.
WARNING: There exist AI generations that may be offensive in nature.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper addresses the safety concerns related to diffusion models (DMs) in generating realistic images, particularly focusing on their vulnerability to generating unsafe content. It introduces a novel adversarial prompt attack named UnlearnDiffAtk, which evaluates the robustness of safety-driven unlearned DMs by demonstrating their inability to effectively erase unwanted concepts, styles, and objects from image generation. The research confirms that existing unlearning techniques are not sufficient and highlights the need for more trustworthy systems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The proposed methodology, UnlearnDiffAtk, leverages the inherent classification capabilities of diffusion models for generating adversarial prompts without relying on auxiliary models. It formulates the problem as a binary classification task, optimizing the perturbations that cause unlearned DMs to generate unsafe images despite their supposed safety guarantees.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves evaluating unlearned DMs on various unlearning tasks (concept, style, object unlearning) using datasets including the I2P dataset (for harmful content) and artistic styles. The attack success rate (ASR) is measured using both the original prompts and adversarial prompts generated by UnlearnDiffAtk, with validation methods involving classifiers to detect unwanted content in generated images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The study acknowledges limitations in the current unlearned DMs, particularly their insufficient robustness against adversarial prompts. Furthermore, the results may not generalize to all possible future models or unique contexts outside the datasets tested. The adversarial prompt generation may also have dependency on the specific diffusion model architecture used in experiments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may explore enhancing the robustness of unlearned DMs to various adversarial attacks, developing more sophisticated safety mechanisms, and extending the evaluation framework to include a broader range of safety scenarios and datasets for more comprehensive testing.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Safeguard Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with Human Feedback Inversion
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
This paper addresses the societal concerns arising from large-scale
text-to-image diffusion models for generating potentially harmful or
copyrighted content. Existing models rely heavily on internet-crawled data,
wherein problematic concepts persist due to incomplete filtration processes.
While previous approaches somewhat alleviate the issue, they often rely on
text-specified concepts, introducing challenges in accurately capturing nuanced
concepts and aligning model knowledge with human understandings. In response,
we propose a framework named Human Feedback Inversion (HFI), where human
feedback on model-generated images is condensed into textual tokens guiding the
mitigation or removal of problematic images. The proposed framework can be
built upon existing techniques for the same purpose, enhancing their alignment
with human judgment. By doing so, we simplify the training objective with a
self-distillation-based technique, providing a strong baseline for concept
removal. Our experimental results demonstrate our framework significantly
reduces objectionable content generation while preserving image quality,
contributing to the ethical deployment of AI in the public sphere.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces a novel framework named Human Feedback Inversion (HFI) to mitigate the generation of harmful or copyrighted content in text-to-image diffusion models. It demonstrates that incorporating human feedback into the model's training process can effectively reduce objectionable content while preserving image quality.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology consists of collecting human feedback on generated images, transforming this feedback into soft tokens for enabling concept removal, and employing a self-distillation-based fine-tuning technique called Safe self-Distillation Diffusion (SDD). HFI utilizes a reward model informed by human ratings to guide the model's training objectives.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize various datasets including prompts from artists and harmful content categories. The model's performance is evaluated using CLIP zero-shot classification to measure the effectiveness and utility of generated images, as well as qualitative evaluations through human feedback. Key benchmarks include the proportion of NSFW content and artistic style retention metrics.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The effectiveness of HFI is contingent upon the quality of the human feedback collected. Significant disagreements in feedback can jeopardize the learning process. Additionally, the model's reliance on inversion techniques presents challenges in capturing nuanced concepts accurately with limited guidance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore refining the feedback collection process, enhancing the reward models, and examining the generalization of HFI and SDD to broader concept categories beyond those currently tested, including more complex or ambiguous concepts.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Enhancing Semantic Fidelity in Text-to-Image Synthesis: Attention
  Regulation in Diffusion Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Recent advancements in diffusion models have notably improved the perceptual
quality of generated images in text-to-image synthesis tasks. However,
diffusion models often struggle to produce images that accurately reflect the
intended semantics of the associated text prompts. We examine cross-attention
layers in diffusion models and observe a propensity for these layers to
disproportionately focus on certain tokens during the generation process,
thereby undermining semantic fidelity. To address the issue of dominant
attention, we introduce attention regulation, a computation-efficient
on-the-fly optimization approach at inference time to align attention maps with
the input text prompt. Notably, our method requires no additional training or
fine-tuning and serves as a plug-in module on a model. Hence, the generation
capacity of the original model is fully preserved. We compare our approach with
alternative approaches across various datasets, evaluation metrics, and
diffusion models. Experiment results show that our method consistently
outperforms other baselines, yielding images that more faithfully reflect the
desired concepts with reduced computation overhead. Code is available at
https://github.com/YaNgZhAnG-V5/attention_regulation.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: This research addresses the problem of semantic fidelity in text-to-image generation using diffusion models. The main contribution is the introduction of an on-the-fly attention regulation technique that modifies cross-attention maps at inference time to promote better alignment with text prompts, thereby enhancing the semantic accuracy of generated images without requiring additional training.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The core method involves a constrained optimization approach applied to the attention maps within diffusion models, specifically targeting cross-attention layers. The optimization aims to equalize attention across relevant tokens and mitigate dominance of any single token, utilizing gradient-based techniques for adjustments while preserving the model's original generation capabilities.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized three datasets: a modified subset of the MS-COCO dataset for general evaluation, an A&E dataset specifically designed to measure semantic issues, and a fine-tuned FID dataset for generative quality assessment. Multiple metrics were used for evaluation including CLIP score, detection success rate, LPIPS score, and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID). Comparisons were made across baseline methods with results indicating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The method may produce unintended results where objects are fused inappropriately, or certain prompts are interpreted incorrectly due to modeling limitations. Additionally, optimization hyperparameters may lead to performance plateaus or over-editing in attention maps, resulting in potential distortion of final generated images.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future exploration could involve improving the interaction and adaptation of attention regulation for more complex image compositions, refining hyperparameter optimization techniques, and assessing the impact on other diffusion model architectures beyond those studied.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Source Prompt Disentangled Inversion for Boosting Image Editability with
  Diffusion Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Text-driven diffusion models have significantly advanced the image editing
performance by using text prompts as inputs. One crucial step in text-driven
image editing is to invert the original image into a latent noise code
conditioned on the source prompt. While previous methods have achieved
promising results by refactoring the image synthesizing process, the inverted
latent noise code is tightly coupled with the source prompt, limiting the image
editability by target text prompts. To address this issue, we propose a novel
method called Source Prompt Disentangled Inversion (SPDInv), which aims at
reducing the impact of source prompt, thereby enhancing the text-driven image
editing performance by employing diffusion models. To make the inverted noise
code be independent of the given source prompt as much as possible, we indicate
that the iterative inversion process should satisfy a fixed-point constraint.
Consequently, we transform the inversion problem into a searching problem to
find the fixed-point solution, and utilize the pre-trained diffusion models to
facilitate the searching process. The experimental results show that our
proposed SPDInv method can effectively mitigate the conflicts between the
target editing prompt and the source prompt, leading to a significant decrease
in editing artifacts. In addition to text-driven image editing, with SPDInv we
can easily adapt customized image generation models to localized editing tasks
and produce promising performance. The source code are available at
https://github.com/leeruibin/SPDInv.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents the Source Prompt Disentangled Inversion (SPDInv) method, aimed at enhancing text-driven image editing by detaching the inverted latent noise code from the source prompt, thereby improving editability and reducing artifacts in the generated images.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: SPDInv reformulates the image inversion process into a fixed-point constraint problem and utilizes pre-trained diffusion models to optimize the inversion without the source prompt prior. It employs iterative optimization to minimize the noise gap between the inverted and ideal noise codes.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments use two datasets: PIE-Bench for general image editing tasks and TDE-Bench for evaluating text-driven editing types. Metrics employed for evaluation include DINO score, PSNR, LPIPS, MSE, SSIM, and CLIP score compared against several state-of-the-art methods.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: SPDInv is limited by its dependency on existing editing engines, inherits their limitations, and struggles with certain tasks like adding and dropping contents, especially in portrait editing. It reduces but does not eliminate the noise gap.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes improving the SPDInv inversion process and developing new editing pipelines to enhance stability and robustness in image editing tasks.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Exploring Attribute Variations in Style-based GANs using Diffusion
  Models
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Existing attribute editing methods treat semantic attributes as binary,
resulting in a single edit per attribute. However, attributes such as
eyeglasses, smiles, or hairstyles exhibit a vast range of diversity. In this
work, we formulate the task of \textit{diverse attribute editing} by modeling
the multidimensional nature of attribute edits. This enables users to generate
multiple plausible edits per attribute. We capitalize on disentangled latent
spaces of pretrained GANs and train a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model
(DDPM) to learn the latent distribution for diverse edits. Specifically, we
train DDPM over a dataset of edit latent directions obtained by embedding image
pairs with a single attribute change. This leads to latent subspaces that
enable diverse attribute editing. Applying diffusion in the highly compressed
latent space allows us to model rich distributions of edits within limited
computational resources. Through extensive qualitative and quantitative
experiments conducted across a range of datasets, we demonstrate the
effectiveness of our approach for diverse attribute editing. We also showcase
the results of our method applied for 3D editing of various face attributes.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: This paper addresses the problem of diverse attribute editing in images generated by GANs by proposing a method that learns the distribution of plausible attribute edits rather than treating attributes as binary. The key contributions include a novel methodology utilizing Diffusion Models to generate multiple variations for attributes like eyeglasses and smiles, and demonstrating effectiveness in both 2D and 3D contexts.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves training a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) over a dataset of edit directions derived from image pairs differing by a single attribute. The approach capitalizes on disentangled latent spaces of pretrained GANs, specifically using the W+ space for capturing the complex distributions of attribute edits and enabling multiple variations during image editing.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using a synthetic dataset generated from CelebA-HQ, which includes 30k source images to create pairs of images for each attribute. The model was evaluated through qualitative and quantitative metrics like FID, cosine similarity, and improved precision and recall across several attributes including hairstyles, smiles, and eyeglasses, with comparisons made against baseline and FLAME methods.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The methodology relies on synthetic image pairs for training, which may restrict the model's effectiveness in real-world scenarios. There is also an implicit assumption that attributes can be distinctly altered in isolation, which may not hold true for all images.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore text-driven multimodal editing, enabling more complex edits that incorporate not just visual attributes but also contextual information. Additionally, fine-grained control over multimodal attribute editing presents an interesting area for further investigation.

{"arxiv_id":"2311.15908v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2311.15908v2","title":"Enhancing Perceptual Quality in Video Super-Resolution through
  Temporally-Consistent Detail Synthesis using Diffusion Models","authors":["Claudio Rota","Marco Buzzelli","Joost van de Weijer"],"published_date":"2023-11-27T15:14:38Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"In this paper, we address the problem of enhancing perceptual quality in
video super-resolution (VSR) using Diffusion Models (DMs) while ensuring
temporal consistency among frames. We present StableVSR, a VSR method based on
DMs that can significantly enhance the perceptual quality of upscaled videos by
synthesizing realistic and temporally-consistent details. We introduce the
Temporal Conditioning Module (TCM) into a pre-trained DM for single image
super-resolution to turn it into a VSR method. TCM uses the novel Temporal
Texture Guidance, which provides it with spatially-aligned and detail-rich
texture information synthesized in adjacent frames. This guides the generative
process of the current frame toward high-quality and temporally-consistent
results. In addition, we introduce the novel Frame-wise Bidirectional Sampling
strategy to encourage the use of information from past to future and
vice-versa. This strategy improves the perceptual quality of the results and
the temporal consistency across frames. We demonstrate the effectiveness of
StableVSR in enhancing the perceptual quality of upscaled videos while
achieving better temporal consistency compared to existing state-of-the-art
methods for VSR. The project page is available at
https://github.com/claudiom4sir/StableVSR.","github_url":"https://github.com/claudiom4sir/StableVSR","main_contributions":"This paper presents StableVSR, a novel approach to video super-resolution that enhances perceptual quality by ensuring temporal consistency among frames, addressing the limitations of existing VSR methods that focus primarily on reconstruction quality.","methodology":"StableVSR is built on Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) and introduces a Temporal Conditioning Module (TCM) that employs Temporal Texture Guidance for synthesizing detail-rich textures from adjacent frames. A Frame-wise Bidirectional Sampling strategy is implemented to propagate information forward and backward across frames.","experimental_setup":"The method was evaluated using two benchmark datasets: Vimeo-90K containing 91701 7-frame video sequences, and REDS, which includes 300 video sequences. Perceptual quality was assessed using metrics like LPIPS and DISTS, alongside traditional metrics such as PSNR and SSIM.","limitations":"StableVSR exhibits a significantly higher model complexity, approximately 35 times greater than compared methods, resulting in increased inference time (about 100 seconds per frame) and higher memory requirements. Improvements in perceptual quality may come at the cost of reconstruction quality, adhering to the perception-distortion trade-off.","future_research_directions":"Future work may focus on reducing sampling steps to enhance inference speed, exploring optimizations for the diffusion models, and investigating other applications of StableVSR in fields requiring high perceptual quality, such as film and gaming."}
